resources.string: A clean XML shell for chore approvals, reward requests, and role-based family flows.
This build now restores Firebase sessions on launch and routes from Firestore family membership instead of a demo role toggle.
Session restore failed. Fix the issue and try again.
Use one shared login form for both family roles. After sign-in, Firestore family membership decides which shell opens.
Parents and children use the same credentials form. Role and family routing come from Firestore after authentication.
If Firebase is not configured in this build yet, sign-in will stay blocked until `google-services.json` or equivalent app options are added.
Create a parent family account or let a child join with a family code from the shared registration screen.
Set up entry to your family
Use one shared screen for both account paths. Parents create the family, children join with a code.
Create the parent account, family record, and first family membership in one flow.
Create a child account and enter the family join code shared by the parent.
Use the 6-character join code stored on the family document.
Password must be at least 6 characters.
This screen now creates real Firebase Auth users and writes the matching Firestore membership records.
Firebase is not configured for this build yet. Add `google-services.json` or fill the runtime Firebase strings in `app/src/main/res/values/firebase_runtime_config.xml`, then reopen the app.
Use the parent dashboard to scan the family workload, review approvals, and jump straight into chore or reward maintenance.
Keep today moving: three chores are still in progress, two requests need approval, and one reward is currently paused.
%1$d chores still need follow-up, %2$d approvals are waiting, and %3$d rewards are active right now.
No backend yet, so this overview is driven by demo content. The structure is ready for live counters and family-specific summaries later.
%1$d chore submissions and %2$d reward requests are waiting for review.
%1$d chores are still open across %2$d child accounts.
No approvals are waiting. %1$d active rewards are available right now.
No approvals or active rewards are waiting right now.
Share this code with a child account so they can join the same family.
No join code was found on this family record yet. Add one in Firestore before demoing child onboarding.
Join my HOM-E family "%1$s" with this code: %2$s
Maya is one task away from her weekly target, Liam submitted a room reset, and the weekend movie reward is still available.
%1$d child accounts are connected with %2$d total points saved across the family.
No child accounts have joined yet. Share the join code when you are ready to connect the first child.
Current chores show the live family list with assignee, status, points, and the existing edit entry points.
No chores have been added for this family yet.
Rewards stay intentionally simple: title, short description, point cost, and whether the reward is active for children right now.
No rewards have been added for this family yet.
Approve finished chores and reward redemption requests from one combined queue. Parent actions update Firestore and keep points balance in sync.
No chore submissions are waiting right now.
No reward requests are waiting right now.
Everything is handled for now. New requests will appear here as children submit chores or request rewards.
This shared form supports both creating and editing a chore with local validation only. Saved values are not persisted yet.
Set up a new chore for the family. Required fields match the frozen parent contract only.
Update the selected chore details and save changes to Firestore.
This shared reward form stays within the frozen scope and validates only the fields the backend will later own.
Create a simple reward with a short description, point cost, and active state.
Adjust the reward details and save changes to Firestore.
A simple child home that shows current points, the most important chores, what is waiting for parent review, and a small rewards peek.
You already have enough points to request %1$s.
Nice work. Your next reward choice is already within reach.
You are caught up for now. New chores will appear here when a parent assigns them.
Keep the home screen light: just the chores that matter most right now.
Submitted chores stay here until a parent reviews them.
Nothing is waiting right now. Finish a chore and submit it from the details screen.
Only active rewards appear here, with one clear request path.
No active rewards are available right now.
My Chores is the main place for browsing assigned work. Open any card to read the full instructions and submit completion.
%1$d chores in your list, %2$d waiting for review.
There are no chores to show yet.
Chore details stay focused on one task: understand what to do, then submit when you are done.
What a finished version looks like
When you tap submit, the chore moves to waiting for review so a parent can check it.
This chore was already submitted. A parent still needs to review it.
Submitted and waiting for parent review.
Rewards stay simple on purpose: active items only, clear cost, and one request action inside reward details.
%1$d points available right now, %2$d reward requests already waiting.
There are no active rewards right now.
Reward details explain what the child is requesting and what happens after the request is sent.
Requesting a reward sends it straight into the parent approval queue.
This reward has already been requested and is now waiting for parent review.
You can still request this reward, but a parent cannot approve it until the points balance is high enough.
Profile keeps the child account screen small and safe: who is signed in, which family they belong to, and sign out.
Child mode stays read-only except for submitting chore completion and requesting rewards.
Example parent row with room for points, assignee, and edit state.
Example child row with room for status, points, and a completion entry point.
Example parent reward row with room for points cost and edit controls.
Example child reward row with room for request state and points cost.
Example queue row for a submitted chore or reward request waiting on parent review.
Complete the form to preview the save state.
Use a whole-number point value for now.
Use a whole-number cost so later backend validation can stay simple.
Choose one child account from the current family.
Turn this off to preview how an inactive reward should read in the list.
Approving a chore marks it reviewed and adds the chore points to the child balance.
Approving a reward request deducts points in the same transaction that updates the request.
Request updated locally for demo purposes.
Reward request sent to the parent queue.
Add a child account before creating a chore.
Enter a whole number greater than 0.
Keep it under 80 characters for the short description.
Only open chores can be edited.
Wipe counters, reload the dishwasher, and leave the sink clear before dinner.
Counters look clear, dishes are loaded, and the sink is ready for the next meal.
Fold the clean laundry pile, sort socks, and place each stack in the right room basket.
The pile is folded into neat stacks and every basket is sorted for the right room.
Refill the water bowl, sweep around the food mat, and put the toy bin back in place.
Water is full, the floor is clean, and the pet corner looks ready again.
Both bins are emptied, liners are replaced, and the kitchen area smells clean.
Make the bed, sort laundry into the basket, and clear the study desk.
Take out the kitchen bins and replace liners before bedtime.
Choose the Friday family movie and the snack pairing.
Good for a cozy weekend night when everyone wants an easy family choice.
Stay up a little later on the weekend after chores are done.
Inactive for now, so it stays out of the store until a parent turns it back on.
Pick the recipe and help lead Saturday dessert prep.
Best when you want a longer reward with hands-on time in the kitchen.
Choose the first board game or video game for family play time.
A smaller reward option that feels reachable after one or two completed chores.
Liam says the room is done and ready for a quick parent check.
Maya marked the dinner dishes complete just before 7:00 PM.
Noa wants to redeem the family movie pick for 45 points.
Maya requested the baking session reward for this weekend.
This area is only available for the parent account on this family.
This area is only available for the child account on this family.
%1$d chores waiting for review, %2$d reward requests waiting.
